5 min read
Just now

--

If you’ve ever run a deploy script twice by accident and held your breath, this post is for you. The word that would have saved you that anxiety is idempotency and it quietly sits at the center of DevOps, SRE, cloud automation, shell scripting, and even how AI agents act on the world.
Link to read full article.
The one-sentence definition
An operation is idempotent if running it once and running it many times produce the same end state.
Turning off a light switch is idempotent — flip it “off” ten times, the light is still just off. Pressing “add one item to cart” is not — do it ten times and you’ve got ten items.
That’s the whole idea. Everything below is just what happens when you take it seriously.
Why it matters more than it sounds
Distributed systems fail in the most inconvenient way: partially. A request reaches the server, the work gets done, and then the network drops before the “success” reply comes back. Your client has no idea whether it worked, so it retries.

• If the operation is idempotent, the retry is harmless. Worst case…
